target.name: alpha-Allene Quaternary Centers in Cyclohexanones
target.motivation: The stereoselective construction of alpha-allene quaternary centers is challenging. This work aims to develop a new strategy using simple monocyclic achiral propargyl vinyl ethers as starting materials.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Asymmetric propargyl Claisen rearrangement of O-propargyl beta-ketoesters using Ni(II)-ligand complex.
Asymmetric propargyl Claisen rearrangement using Co(II)-ligand complex.
Propargyl Claisen rearrangement as a critical step in the total synthesis of azadirachtin.

success_criteria.free_text: Enantio- and diastereoselective synthesis of alpha-allene quaternary centers in fully substituted cyclohexanones at the alpha-positions.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond formation at the alpha-position of the cyclohexanone and allene formation
key_disconnections.reasoning: The propargyl Claisen rearrangement of propargyl vinyl ethers yields an alpha-allene functionality. The authors proposed that a Grignard addition to 2-O-propargyl enones would produce a magnesium-chelate intermediate, which would then activate the [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement, guiding diastereoselectivity and forming the alpha-allene quaternary centers.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy involves a cascade sequence of 1,2-carbonyl addition and propargyl Claisen rearrangement. The initial Grignard addition to 2-O-propargyl enones forms a magnesium alkoxide chelate intermediate, which then activates the propargyl vinyl ether moiety, triggering the cascade rearrangement in a diastereoselective manner. The enantioselective version is achieved by regulating the facial selectivity of the Grignard addition using a chiral ligand.

thought.background: The Claisen rearrangement is a powerful method for carbon-carbon bond creation and constructing carbon quaternary centers. A variant, the propargyl Claisen rearrangement, converts propargyl vinyl ethers to alpha-allene functionalities. While examples of diastereoselective synthesis of alpha-allene quaternary centers via propargyl Claisen rearrangement exist, they often involve complex substrates or harsh conditions.
thought.gap: There is a need for a new strategy for the stereoselective synthesis of alpha-allene quaternary centers using simple monocyclic achiral propargyl vinyl ethers.
thought.decision: To achieve stereoselective synthesis of alpha-allene quaternary centers, we propose treating 2-O-propargyl enone 5 with a Grignard reagent. This is expected to produce a magnesium-chelate intermediate 7 via 1,2-carbonyl addition, which will then activate a cascade propargyl Claisen rearrangement, guiding diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: Hypothesis formed for a cascade Grignard addition/propargyl Claisen rearrangement.

evidence.text_span: The limited examples of diastereoselective synthesis of α- allene quaternary centers via propargyl Claisen rearrangement prompted us to explore a new strategy with a focus on using simple monocyclic achiral propargyl vinyl ethers, specifically 2- O-propargyl enone 5, as starting materials. We proposed that treatment of substrate 5 with a Grignard reagent would produce a magnesium-chelate intermediate 7 as a product of the 1,2-carbonyl addition reaction. This chelation would then activate the [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement, thereby forming the α-allene quaternary centers while also guiding diastereoselectivity.

thought.background: The hypothesis is that a Grignard addition to 2-O-propargyl enone 5a, followed by warming, will trigger a propargyl Claisen rearrangement to form alpha-allene ketone 6a.
thought.gap: Determine suitable solvent conditions for the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To test the hypothesis and find an effective solvent, methylmagnesium bromide was introduced to a precooled solution of 2-O-propargyl enone 5a in THF, Et2O, and DCM. The reaction mixture was then warmed to room temperature to initiate the rearrangement, expecting to form 6a.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Product 6a was formed, but the yield was not specified for THF. DCM was found to be effective.

evidence.text_span: To test our hypothesis, we introduced methylmagnesium bromide to a precooled solution of 2-O-propargyl enone 5a in three different solvents, i.e., THF, Et2O, and DCM. Once the substrate was fully consumed, the reaction mixture was warmed to room temperature, which triggered the propargyl Claisen rearrangement to furnish α-allene ketone 6a. Interestingly, DCM was found to be effective in generating the product in 82% yield as a single diastereomer.

thought.background: Initial experiments showed that methylmagnesium bromide with 2-O-propargyl enone 5a in DCM produced 6a. The goal is to optimize the reaction conditions.
thought.gap: Determine the optimal temperature for the Grignard addition.
thought.decision: To optimize the reaction, the Grignard addition was conducted at 0 °C and -78 °C, expecting to find conditions that afford cleaner crude reaction mixtures.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: DCM was effective, yielding 82% of 6a as a single diastereomer. Lower temperatures (0 and -78 °C) afforded cleaner crude reaction mixtures, with comparable product yields. No overaddition to the emerging ketone functionality was observed with a slight excess of Grignard reagent.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, DCM was found to be effective in generating the product in 82% yield as a single diastereomer. We then explored the temperature effects (0 and −78 °C) for the Grignard addition. Although the product yields were comparable, the lower temperature afforded cleaner crude reaction mixtures. Remarkably, the use of a slight excess of the Grignard reagent (1.3 equiv), which necessitated full consumption of substrate 5, did not lead to overaddition to the emerging ketone functionality in 6a.

thought.background: The racemic conditions for the cascade Grignard addition/propargyl Claisen rearrangement have been established for 5a. The next step is to explore the scope of the reaction by varying the R substituent in the enone moiety.
thought.gap: Determine the applicability of the method to different R substituents.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, substrates with octyl, isobutyl, benzyl, phenyl, 4-OMe-phenyl, 4-F-phenyl, and 3-benzothiophene groups as R substituents were treated with methylmagnesium bromide under the established racemic conditions, expecting to form products 6b-6h.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Products 6b to 6d (octyl, isobutyl, benzyl) were formed in moderate to good yields (45-74%). Aromatic substituents (phenyl, 4-OMe-phenyl, 4-F-phenyl, 3-benzothiophene) yielded 6e-6h in 65-75% yields. Some aromatic rings required warming to reflux for the propargyl Claisen rearrangement.

evidence.text_span: Our survey began with varying the R substituent in the enone moiety. When treated with methylmagnesium bromide, substrates containing long-chain octyl, branched isobutyl, and benzyl groups formed products 6b to 6d in moderate to good yields (entries 2−4). We then proceeded with aromatic substituents, including phenyl, 4- OMe-phenyl, 4-F-phenyl, and 3-benzothiophene (entries 5− 8). In these cases, the resulting α-allene ketones 6e-6h were isolated in 65-75% yields. Notably, certain aromatic rings seemed to affect the propargyl Claisen rearrangement, requiring warming the reaction mixtures to reflux.

thought.background: The racemic conditions are applicable to various R substituents. Now, the focus is on varying the alkyne substituents (Ra).
thought.gap: Determine the effect of different alkyne substituents on the reaction outcome.
thought.decision: To explore the scope, methyl, phenyl, 2-thiophene, and bromo groups were introduced at the Ra position of the alkyne, expecting to form products 6i-6l under the established racemic conditions.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Ketones 6i-6l were isolated with 60-83% yields, demonstrating tolerance for various alkyne substituents.

evidence.text_span: Continuing with the alkyne substituents, methyl, phenyl, 2-thiophene, and bromo groups were introduced at the Ra position, which decorated the α-allene group. As indicated in entries 9−12, ketones 6i−6l were isolated with 60−83% yields.

thought.background: The racemic conditions have been explored for varying R and Ra substituents. The next step is to examine the scope of Grignard reagents (Rb).
thought.gap: Determine the compatibility of different Grignard reagents with the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, organomagnesium reagents bearing octyl, cyclohexyl, benzyl, allyl, vinyl, and 1-propynyl groups were examined under the established racemic conditions, expecting to form products 6m-6r.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Octyl, cyclohexyl, benzyl, and allyl Grignard reagents were tolerated, furnishing 6m-6p in high yields (55-84%). Benzyl-containing product 6o required reflux conditions. Vinylmagnesium bromide resulted in 6q in 70% yield, while 1-propynyl counterpart formed 6r with a lower yield (41%).

evidence.text_span: Lastly, various Grignard reagents were also examined (entries 13−20). Organomagnesium reagents bearing octyl, cyclohexyl, benzyl, and allyl groups were tolerated, furnishing α-allene ketones 6m-6p in high yields. For benzyl-containing product 6o, the propargyl Claisen rearrangement required reflux conditions. We noted differing reactivities between sp2 and sp hybridized nucleophiles. While vinylmagnesium bromide resulted in product 6q in 70% yield, the 1-propynyl counterpart formed 6r with a lower yield.

thought.background: The racemic conditions have been explored for various Grignard reagents. Phenylmagnesium bromide is being tested.
thought.gap: Determine if phenylmagnesium bromide is compatible with the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, phenylmagnesium bromide was examined under the established racemic conditions, expecting to form product 6s.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Phenylmagnesium bromide only yielded the 1,2-carbonyl addition product, and the propargyl Claisen rearrangement did not occur.

evidence.text_span: An unexpected outcome was noted when comparing phenyl and 4-F-phenyl. While 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide formed α-allene ketone 6t in 61% yield, the phenyl variant only yielded the 1,2-carbonyl addition product. In fact, the propargyl Claisen rearrangement did not occur.

thought.background: Phenylmagnesium bromide failed to induce the propargyl Claisen rearrangement. However, 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide showed promise.
thought.gap: Confirm the compatibility of 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide with the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide was examined under the established racemic conditions, expecting to form product 6t.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide formed alpha-allene ketone 6t in 61% yield.

evidence.text_span: While 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide formed α-allene ketone 6t in 61% yield, the phenyl variant only yielded the 1,2-carbonyl addition product. In fact, the propargyl Claisen rearrangement did not occur.

thought.background: The racemic synthesis conditions have been established. The next goal is to develop an enantioselective version of the method by regulating the facial selectivity of the Grignard addition.
thought.gap: Identify a suitable chiral ligand and solvent for asymmetric 1,2-carbonyl addition.
thought.decision: To achieve enantioselectivity, methylmagnesium bromide (3.9 equiv) was reacted with substrate 5a in the presence of 9-anthracyl-H8-BINOL ligand 7a (1.4 equiv) at -78 °C. The reaction was conducted in toluene and DCM to evaluate solvent effects on enantioinduction, expecting to form (+)-6a.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: In toluene, 64:36 er was observed. In DCM, a stronger enantioinduction of 81:19 er was achieved. In both cases, (+)-6a was formed as a single diastereomer. Yields were not quantified due to product volatility.

evidence.text_span: The pilot experiment involved the reaction of methylmagnesium bromide (3.9 equiv) to substrate 5a in the presence of 9-anthracyl- H8-BINOL ligand 7a (1.4 equiv) at −78 °C. Once the starting material was consumed, the mixtures were allowed to warm to room temperature to initiate the propargyl Claisen rearrangement. We conducted these reactions in two noncoordinating solvents, i.e., toluene and DCM (entries 1 and 2), and found that the use of DCM led to stronger enantioinduction (81:19 er) compared to toluene (64:36 er). Equally significant, the resulting product (+)-6a was formed as a single diastereomer.

thought.background: DCM was identified as a better solvent for enantioinduction. The next step is to screen various H8-BINOL ligands to further improve enantioselectivity.
thought.gap: Identify a more effective chiral ligand for higher enantioinduction.
thought.decision: To optimize enantioselectivity, various H8-BINOL ligands (7b-7k) with different substituents (9-phenanthryl, 1-pyrenyl, smaller substituents, 3,5-(CF3)2Ph, 4-OMePh, 2-OMePh, 3,4-dimethoxy, 4-NMe2Ph) were screened in DCM at -78 °C, expecting to find a ligand that provides higher er for (+)-6a.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Ligands 7b and 7c (9-phenanthryl, 1-pyrenyl) marginally improved er (87:13, 88:12). Smaller substituents (7d-7f) showed similar trends, with phenyl group in 7f giving 86:14 er. 3,5-(CF3)2Ph in 7g eroded enantioselectivity (72:27 er). 4-OMePh in 7h improved er to 93:7. 2-OMePh in 7i dropped er to 68:32. 3,4-dimethoxy in 7j resulted in a racemic mixture (50:50 er). 4-NMe2Ph in 7k gave comparable er to 7f (87:13). PMP-H8-BINOL 7h was identified as the most effective ligand.

evidence.text_span: This promising outcome prompted us to screen various substituents in the H8−BINOL ligands. Other bulky groups, such as 9-phenanthryl 7b and 1-pyrenyl 7c, only marginally improved enantioselectivity (entries 3 and 4). Interestingly, similar trends were noted with smaller substituents in ligands 7d−7f (entries 5−7), with the most intriguing result arising from a simple phenyl group in ligand 7f, which afforded the α- allene ketone with 86:14 er. The surprising efficacy of the phenyl group prompted an evaluation of its stereoelectronic effects. As shown in entries 8 and 9, the inclusion of 3,5-(CF3)2Ph groups in ligand 7g resulted in an erosion of enantioselectivity. In contrast, 4- OMePh in ligand 7h improved er to 93:7. The positioning of the methoxy group within the phenyl ring is crucial. For example, 2-OMePh in ligand 7i (entry 10) dropped enantioselectivity to 68:32. Efforts to enhance enantioinduction by incorporating two methoxy groups at the 3,4-positions in ligand 7j proved ineffective, as the resulting α-allene ketone was formed as a racemic mixture (entry 11). We also examined the 4-NMe2Ph variant, which yielded enantioselectivity comparable to that of the parent ligand 7f (entry 12). Overall, our ligand screening identified PMP-H8-BINOL 7h as the most effective ligand.

thought.background: PMP-H8-BINOL 7h was identified as the most effective ligand, yielding 93:7 er at -78 °C.
thought.gap: Further enhance enantioselectivity.
thought.decision: To further enhance enantioinduction, the reaction temperature was lowered to -90 °C with PMP-H8-BINOL 7h, expecting to achieve higher er for (+)-6a.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Lowering the reaction temperature to -90 °C produced (+)-6a with 98:2 er.

evidence.text_span: The enantioselectivity was further enhanced by lowering the reaction temperature to −90 °C. Under these conditions, the α-allene quaternary center in ketone (+)-6a was produced with 98:2 er (entry 13).

thought.background: Optimized enantioselective conditions (PMP-H8-BINOL 7h, -90 °C in DCM) have been established for 5a. The next step is to survey the scope of the reaction by assessing the effects of the R substituent on the enone moiety.
thought.gap: Determine the applicability of the optimized enantioselective method to different R substituents.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, substrates featuring octyl, isobutyl, benzyl, phenyl, 4-OMe-phenyl, 4-F-phenyl, and 3-benzothiophene groups as R substituents were treated with methylmagnesium bromide under the optimized enantioselective conditions, expecting to form products (+)-6b to (+)-6h with high er and dr.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Octyl and isobutyl groups afforded (+)-6b and (+)-6c in 98% and 82% yields, respectively, with 92:8 and 93:7 er. Benzyl variant yielded (+)-6d in 77% yield with 94:6 er. Aromatic and heteroaromatic substituents: (+)-6e, (+)-6g, and (+)-6h were obtained with good yields and enantioselectivity. (-)-6f (4-OMe-phenyl) was isolated with 72:28 er. Some substrates required reflux conditions.

evidence.text_span: Using these optimized conditions, we surveyed the scope of reaction by first assessing effects of the R substituent on the enone moiety with methylmagnesium bromide as the nucleophile (Scheme 3). Substrates featuring octyl and isobutyl groups afforded α-allene ketone (+)-6b and (+)-6c in 98% and 82% yields, respectively, with comparable 92:8 and 93:7 er. Similarly, the benzyl variant yielded product (+)-6d in 77% yield with a 94:6 er. Next, we examined aromatic and heteroaromatic substituents. Except the 4-OMe-phenyl in (-)-6f, which was isolated with 72:28 er, the phenyl (+)-6e, the 4-F-phenyl (+)-6g, and the 3-benzothiophene (+)-6h were obtained with good yields and enantioselectivity.

thought.background: The optimized enantioselective conditions are effective for varying R substituents. The next step is to explore the effect of substitutions at the Ra position of the resulting alpha-allene functionality.
thought.gap: Determine the compatibility of different Ra substituents with the optimized enantioselective method.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, methyl, phenyl, 2-thiophene, and bromo groups were introduced at the Ra position, expecting to form alpha-allene ketones (-)-6i to (+)-6l with good yields and enantioselectivity under the optimized conditions.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Alpha-allene ketones (-)-6i to (+)-6l were isolated in 72-93% yields with er ranging between 87:13 and 92:8. Some substrates required reflux conditions.

evidence.text_span: Efforts to place substitutions at the Ra position of the resulting α-allene functionality with methyl, phenyl, 2-thiophene, and bromo were successful. As depicted in α-allene ketones (−)-6i to (+)-6l, these products were isolated in 72-93% yields with er ranging between 87:13 and 92:8. As detailed in Supporting Information, the absolute and relative stereochemistry of these enantiomerically enriched products was deduced by analogy using X-ray crystallography of (+)-6g, (+)-6j, and (+)-6l. Similar to the racemic synthesis, the propargyl Claisen rearrangement for some substrates required reflux conditions to proceed.

thought.background: The optimized enantioselective conditions are effective with methylmagnesium bromide. The next step is to explore the use of larger nucleophiles.
thought.gap: Determine if larger Grignard reagents are compatible with the enantioselective cascade reaction without losing enantioinduction.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, octylmagnesium bromide, benzylmagnesium bromide, and allylmagnesium bromide were employed under the optimized enantioselective conditions, expecting to form products (+)-6m, (+/-)-6o, and (+/-)-6p with high er and dr.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Octylmagnesium bromide yielded (+)-6m with 69:31 er, indicating a loss in enantioinduction. Benzyl and allyl counterparts formed (+/-)-6o and (+/-)-6p as racemic mixtures in low yields.

evidence.text_span: While the asymmetric conditions proved effective with methylmagnesium bromide, efforts to employ larger nucleophiles led to a loss in enantioinduction. For instance, octylmagnesium bromide yielded product (+)-6m with 69:31 er. Surprisingly, the benzyl and allyl counterparts formed α-allene ketones (±)-6o and (±)-6p as a racemic mixture in low yields.

thought.background: Larger nucleophiles led to a loss in enantioinduction. Aromatic nucleophiles are being tested.
thought.gap: Determine if phenylmagnesium bromide is compatible with the enantioselective cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, phenylmagnesium bromide was examined under the optimized enantioselective conditions, expecting to form product (+)-6s.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Phenylmagnesium bromide did not lead to the propargyl Claisen rearrangement, consistent with the racemic synthesis.

evidence.text_span: We also assessed aromatic nucleophiles, such as phenyl and 4-F-phenyl. While phenylmagnesium bromide did not lead to the propargyl Claisen rearrangement, which is consistent with the racemic synthesis (Scheme 2, entry 19), the 4-F-phenyl adduct (+)-6t was isolated in 70% yield with 69:31 er.

thought.background: Phenylmagnesium bromide failed in the enantioselective conditions. However, 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide showed promise in the racemic synthesis.
thought.gap: Determine if 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide is compatible with the enantioselective cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, 4-F-phenylmagnesium bromide was examined under the optimized enantioselective conditions, expecting to form product (+)-6t.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: The 4-F-phenyl adduct (+)-6t was isolated in 70% yield with 69:31 er.

evidence.text_span: We also assessed aromatic nucleophiles, such as phenyl and 4-F-phenyl. While phenylmagnesium bromide did not lead to the propargyl Claisen rearrangement, which is consistent with the racemic synthesis (Scheme 2, entry 19), the 4-F-phenyl adduct (+)-6t was isolated in 70% yield with 69:31 er.

thought.background: The enantioselective method has been developed. The next step is to demonstrate its utility in the context of complex molecules.
thought.gap: Apply the method to a complex, polycyclic substrate like formestane.
thought.decision: To demonstrate utility, formestane-derived substrate (+)-8 was functionalized with methylmagnesium bromide (2.6 equiv) in DCM at -78 °C to reflux, expecting diastereoselective 1,2-carbonyl addition at both C3 and C17 positions and alpha-allene group installation at C5, forming (+)-9a.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Product (+)-9a was isolated in 66% yield as a single diastereomer, with diastereoselective 1,2-carbonyl addition at both C3 and C17 positions and alpha-allene group installation at C5.

evidence.text_span: The utility of this magnesium-chelate activation of propargyl Claisen rearrangement within the context of complex molecules was exemplified through the functionalization of formestane-derived substrate (+)-8 with methylmagnesium bromide. As shown in the resulting product (+)-9a, which was isolated in 66% yield as a single diastereomer, the reaction of (+)-8 with 2.6 equiv of the Grignard reagent led to diastereoselective 1,2-carbonyl addition at both C3 and C17 positions, while installing the α-allene group at the C5 carbon.

thought.background: Functionalization of formestane-derived substrate (+)-8 with 2.6 equiv of methylmagnesium bromide resulted in addition at both C3 and C17. The goal is to achieve chemoselective addition at C3.
thought.gap: Achieve chemoselective Grignard addition at the C3 carbonyl of (+)-8, followed by propargyl Claisen rearrangement.
thought.decision: To achieve chemoselectivity, substrate (+)-8 was subjected to 1.1 equiv of methylmagnesium bromide in DCM at -78 °C to reflux, expecting methyl addition at the C3 carbonyl, followed by propargyl Claisen rearrangement to furnish alpha-allene ketone (-)-9b.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Methyl addition occurred at the C3 carbonyl, followed by propargyl Claisen rearrangement, furnishing alpha-allene ketone (-)-9b in 40% yield as a single diastereomer. This chemoselectivity was attributed to the chelation effect between magnesium and the neighboring oxygen atoms at C3 and C4.

evidence.text_span: Subjecting the substrate with 1.1 equiv of methylmagnesium bromide remarkably led to methyl addition at the C3 carbonyl, followed by the propargyl Claisen rearrangement to furnish α-allene ketone (-)-9b in 40% yield, which was isolated as a single diastereomer. This intriguing chemoselectivity was most likely the result of the chelation effect between the magnesium metal and the two neighboring oxygen atoms at the C3 and C4 positions, which consequently differentiated the rate of the Grignard addition at the C3 carbonyl compared to that of the C17.

thought.background: Both racemic and enantioselective protocols have been developed for the synthesis of alpha-allene ketones. The next step is to demonstrate the practicality of the method through scale-up reactions.
thought.gap: Perform scale-up reactions for substrate 5j using both racemic and enantioselective protocols.
thought.decision: To demonstrate practicality, scale-up reactions of substrate 5j were carried out on a one-gram quantity using both racemic and enantioselective protocols, expecting to produce 6j and (+)-6j in high yields and stereoselectivity.

reaction.named_reaction: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Racemic conditions furnished 6j in 83% yield with >20:1 dr. Enantioselective version produced (+)-6j in 93% yield with 94:6 er and >20:1 dr. PMP-H8-BINOL ligand 7h was recovered in 91% mass recovery.

evidence.text_span: Next, we carried out scale-up reactions of substrate 5j on a one-gram quantity using both racemic and enantioselective protocols. As expected, the racemic conditions furnished α-allene ketone 6j in 83% yield with >20:1 dr. The enantioselective version also proceeded smoothly to produce (+)-6j in 93% yield with 94:6 er and >20:1 dr. Significantly, the PMP-H8-BINOL ligand 7h was successfully recovered in pure form with a mass recovery of 91%, thus underscoring the cost-effectiveness and practicality of our method.

thought.background: The alpha-allene ketone functionality in product 6j has been successfully synthesized. The next step is to demonstrate its synthetic elaboration to create stereochemically complex derivatives.
thought.gap: Transform 6j into more complex structures through carbonyl reduction and intramolecular cyclization.
thought.decision: To demonstrate synthetic elaboration, 6j was subjected to carbonyl reduction with NaBH4 to produce stereotriad 10, followed by intramolecular cyclization catalyzed by AgOTf to yield heterocycle 11. Both racemic and enantioenriched substrates were used, expecting to generate their respective products in high yields.

reaction.named_reaction: Carbonyl reduction

outcome.observation: Carbonyl reduction of 6j with NaBH4 produced stereotriad 10. Intramolecular cyclization of 10 catalyzed by AgOTf effectively yielded heterocycle 11 in 87% yield. Both racemic and enantioenriched substrates generated their respective products in comparably high yields.

evidence.text_span: The α-allene ketone functionality in product 6j was then subjected to further synthetic elaboration to create stereochemically complex derivatives. For example, carbonyl reduction of 6j with NaBH4 produced stereotriad 10, where the diastereochemical outcome was directed by the hydroxy stereocenter at the opposing α-position. Following this step, intramolecular cyclization catalyzed by AgOTf effectively yielded heterocycle 11. Both racemic and enantioenriched substrate (±)-6j and (+)-6j were subjected to this sequence, generating their respective products in comparably high yields.

thought.background: Heterocycle 11 has been successfully synthesized from 6j. The next step is to further functionalize this heterocycle.
thought.gap: Cleave the electron-rich double bond in heterocycle 11 oxidatively.
thought.decision: To further functionalize, the electron-rich double bond in heterocycle 11 was cleaved oxidatively using catalytic RuCl3 and NaIO4 in wet reaction medium, expecting to afford highly functionalized product 12. Both racemic and enantioenriched substrates were used, expecting to generate their respective products in high yields.

reaction.named_reaction: Oxidative cleavage

outcome.observation: Oxidative cleavage afforded highly functionalized product 12 in 70% yield. Both racemic and enantioenriched substrates generated their respective products in comparably high yields.

evidence.text_span: The electron-rich double bond in the resulting ring structure could then be cleaved oxidatively using catalytic RuCl3 and NaIO4 in wet reaction medium, which afforded highly functionalized product 12. Both racemic and enantioenriched substrate (±)-6j and (+)-6j were subjected to this sequence, generating their respective products in comparably high yields.

thought.background: The method has been successfully applied to cyclohexanone derivatives and formestane. The next step is to test its applicability to a heterocyclic substrate motif.
thought.gap: Determine if the enantioselective cascade reaction is effective for a pyranone-based substrate.
thought.decision: To assess the scope, heterocyclic substrate motif 13 was subjected to the enantioselective conditions (MeMgBr, PMP-H8-BINOL 7h in DCM at -90 °C to rt), expecting to form pyranone (+)-14.

reaction.named_reaction: Asymmetric Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement

outcome.observation: Pyranone (+)-14 was formed as a single diastereomer in 73% yield, albeit with a lower enantioinduction of 68:32 er compared to the cyclohexanone counterpart (+)-6g.

evidence.text_span: We also subjected our method to heterocyclic substrate motif 13, which formed pyranone (+)-14 as a single diastereomer, albeit with 68:32 er. This enantioinduction is surprisingly lower than that of the cyclohexanone counterpart (+)-6g.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The developed method provides a new strategy for the enantio- and diastereoselective synthesis of alpha-allene quaternary centers using simple monocyclic achiral propargyl vinyl ethers, which is an advancement over existing methods that often require complex substrates or harsh conditions.
validation.significance: This work provides a highly efficient and stereoselective method for constructing alpha-allene quaternary centers in fully substituted cyclohexanones. The cascade Grignard addition/propargyl Claisen rearrangement sequence is robust and applicable to complex molecules, with the added benefit of ligand recovery, making it cost-effective and practical. The method's utility was demonstrated through synthetic elaborations, highlighting its potential for creating stereochemically complex derivatives.

source.title: Cascade Grignard Addition: Propargyl Claisen Rearrangement for the Stereoselective Synthesis of α-Allene Quaternary Centers in Cyclohexanones.
source.authors: Armendariz-Gonzalez E, Saputra A, Mureka EW, Locicero CM, Womble GL, Tan G, Watson AA, Fronczek FR, Kartika R.
